title: Annona calcarata
taxonomy: Annona calcarata is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Annonaceae, commonly known as the custard apple family. This family consists of around 160 genera and over 2,000 species. Annona calcarata is native to Madagascar and is part of the genus Annona, which comprises around 150 species. The genus Annona is further divided into two subgenera: Annona and Oxandra. The classification of Annona calcarata reflects its unique characteristics and relationships within the Annonaceae family.
uses: Annona calcarata is a plant species native to Madagascar, and it has been studied for its potential medicinal properties. Its seeds have been found to contain annonaceous acetogenins, which are compounds with anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative activities. Research suggests that extracts from Annona calcarata may have antitumor and antimicrobial properties, making it a subject of interest for further studies. However, more research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal uses and potential applications. The plant's seeds are also used in traditional medicine in Madagascar to treat various ailments, but the effectiveness of these treatments is not well documented.

intro: Annona calcarata, also known as the Calcarata Annona or the Suckling Annona, is a tropical plant species belonging to the Annonaceae family, native to Madagascar. The fruit and other parts of the plant are used in traditional medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal issues, due to its reported health benefits in reducing inflammation and improving digestive function. The bioactive constituents of Annona calcarata include annonaceous acetogenins, which have been found to exhibit cytotoxic and anti-inflammatory properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Annona calcarata include teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as dried extracts and powdered forms, which are often used in traditional medicine to treat a range of health conditions.
